message:
chore(beads): close EPIC git_safety_guard-nqhi

All 8 children shipped (.1 truncate, .2 shred, .3 unlink, .4 redirect,
.5 dd, .6 tar, .7 mv, .8 promote system.disk). Recursive-force-delete
bypass surface is now closed for the canonical sibling commands.

Two follow-up beads spawned during the work:
- 33xf: cross-segment data-flow analyzer (cp/ln/rsync chains)
- 8kh4: mkswap coverage gap in system.disk

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id": "git_safety_guard-nax", "title": "[CATEGORY] search.* - Search Engine Pack Suite", "description": "# Search Engine Pack Suite

## Overview
This category covers protection for search and analytics engines. Index deletion, mapping changes, or cluster modifications can cause significant data loss and extended downtime for search functionality.

## Packs to Implement

### search.elasticsearch
- **Why Critical**: Elasticsearch powers search for countless applications. Index deletion is instantaneous and permanent. Mapping changes can corrupt data.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `curl -X DELETE .../index` - Index deletion via REST
  - `DELETE /_all` - Deletes ALL indices
  - `DELETE /*` - Wildcard deletion
  - `POST /_reindex` with destructive dest - Overwrites destination
  - `PUT /_cluster/settings` (dangerous settings)
  - `POST /_close` - Closes indices (makes unavailable)
  - Kibana/DevTools equivalents
- **Safe Patterns**: `GET`, `_search`, `_cat`, `_cluster/health`

### search.opensearch
- **Why Critical**: AWS-managed fork of Elasticsearch with same risks. Many orgs migrated to OpenSearch.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**: Same as Elasticsearch plus:
  - `aws opensearch delete-domain` - Deletes entire domain
  - `aws opensearch delete-inbound-connection`
  - OpenSearch Dashboards destructive operations
- **Safe Patterns**: `describe-`, `list-`, `get-`

### search.algolia
- **Why Critical**: SaaS search with API-based management. Index deletion affects all applications using that index.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `algolia indices delete` - Index deletion
  - `algolia indices clear` - Clears all records
  - `algolia rules delete` - Deletes search rules
  - `algolia synonyms delete` - Deletes synonyms
  - API calls with `deleteIndex`, `clearObjects`
- **Safe Patterns**: `browse`, `search`, `list`, `get`

### search.meilisearch
- **Why Critical**: Growing alternative to Algolia/Elasticsearch. Same index deletion risks.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `curl -X DELETE .../indexes/` - Index deletion
  - `DELETE /indexes/{uid}/documents` - Document deletion
  - `POST /dumps` (with overwrite)
- **Safe Patterns**: `GET`, `/search`, `/stats`

## Rationale
Search indices often take hours or days to rebuild from source data. During that time, search functionality is degraded or unavailable. Some search systems (especially SaaS) don't have point-in-time recovery, making deletion truly permanent.

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 1 Epic (git_safety_guard-6ae)
- Blocked by: None (can start immediately)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All four packs implemented with comprehensive patterns
- [ ] REST API patterns detected in curl/httpie commands
- [ ] CLI tool patterns for each search engine
- [ ] Tests cover wildcard deletion patterns
- [ ] Documentation warns about index rebuild times", "status": "closed", "priority": 0, "issue_type": "feature", "created_at": "2026-01-09T19:27:17.807248038Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at":  {"id": "git_safety_guard-nimk", "title": "[Epic 1] Pattern Testing Tool (dcg test)", "description": "## EPIC 1: Pattern Testing Tool (dcg test)

### Background & Context
dcg is a configuration-driven security tool. Users define allowlists, select packs, adjust confidence thresholds, and set severity levels. However, there is currently NO way to validate this configuration without actually running commands and observing whether they are blocked.

This is a fundamental gap. It's equivalent to writing code without being able to run tests. Users cannot:
- Verify their allowlists work as intended
- Debug why a command was blocked (or not blocked)
- Test configuration changes before deploying them
- Validate CI/CD pipeline configurations

### Goals & Objectives
1. Enable users to test any command string against dcg's full evaluation pipeline
2. Show detailed breakdown of what matched (pattern name, pack, severity, confidence)
3. Provide machine-readable output (JSON) for scripting and CI integration
4. Support testing against specific config files (--config flag)

### Reasoning & Justification
This is ranked #1 because it's FOUNDATIONAL to dcg's usability:
- Every config-driven tool needs a validation/testing mechanism
- Reduces user frustration and support burden
- Enables confident configuration changes
- Makes dcg predictable and debuggable

### Implementation Considerations
- Entry point: Add 'test' subcommand in src/cli.rs
- Reuse existing evaluation logic from src/evaluator.rs
- Output formatting: Create detailed human-readable format in src/output/
- Support --json flag for machine-readable output
- Consider --verbose flag for even more detail (keyword matches, regex attempts)
- Should work without affecting history database (read-only evaluation)

### Success Criteria
- 'dcg test "rm -rf /"' shows exactly which pattern blocked it
- 'dcg test "npm run build"' shows it's allowed (and why - no match or allowlist)
- 'dcg test --json "command"' outputs structured JSON for CI
- Users can confidently modify config and verify behavior

### Related Codebase Files
- src/cli.rs (add subcommand)
- src/evaluator.rs (evaluation logic to expose)
- src/output/denial.rs (output formatting patterns)
- src/config.rs (config loading for --config flag)

### Dependencies
- None - this is foundational

### Project Goal Alignment
This serves dcg's mission of being an "ally, not adversary" by making the tool transparent and debuggable. Users who understand why things are blocked trust the tool more and are less likely to disable it.", "status": "closed", "priority": 1, "issue_type": "epic", "owner": "jeff141421@gmail.com", "created_at": "2026-01-18T18:09:29.326988836Z", "created_by": "Dicklesworthstone", "updated_at": "2026-01-19T08:39:12.262851167Z", "closed_at": "2026-01-19T08:39:12.262762219Z", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "labels": ["core"]}
 {"id": "git_safety_guard-nmi6", "title": "[EPIC] Allow-Once UX Improvements", "notes": "## Summary

Improve the allow-once bypass code user experience. The current system works but users report they've NEVER SEEN the codes, indicating a critical visibility problem.

## Problem Statement

When dcg blocks a command, it outputs a short code that enables temporary bypass. However:
1. The code uses hex characters (harder to type than numbers)
2. The code is not visually prominent in terminal output
3. Users don't notice it, making the safety-valve mechanism useless

## Child Issues

- git_safety_guard-z72c: Switch to numeric-only codes (5 digits)
- git_safety_guard-3dg8: Add bold/color formatting for visibility

## Impact

If users can't easily see/use bypass codes:
- They get frustrated with 'false positives'
- They disable dcg entirely
- The safety-valve design fails

Making codes visible and easy to use is critical for adoption.

## No Dependencies

These are standalone UX improvements that can be done in any order or in parallel.", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "feature", "created_at": "2026-01-14T06:24:45.984797292Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-01-15T21:17:43.489909027Z", "closed_at": "2026-01-15T21:17:43.489909027Z", "close_reason": "All child issues completed: numeric-only codes (z72c) and bold/color formatting (3dg8). Allow-once UX now features easy-to-type 5-digit numeric codes with prominent visual formatting.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","title":"EPIC: Block all destructive command families equivalent to recursive force-delete","description":"Parent epic tracking dcg's coverage of bytewise-equivalent or near-equivalent destruction primitives that bypass the rm-recursive-force protection. find -delete is now closed (commit ea7e1db); this epic tracks the remaining classes.

Each child bead covers ONE command class (one binary or syntax form), with its own focused regex patterns, suggestion entries, unit tests, and e2e test script. The pattern is identical to find-delete: block at Critical when target is sensitive (root/home/system dir), High otherwise, with safe whitelist for temp dirs.

Children are tracked via parent dependency. Close this epic only when ALL children close.","design":"## Architecture

Each child follows the find-delete model in `src/packs/core/filesystem.rs`:

1. Pack `keywords` array updated to include the new binary name (e.g. `"shred"`, `"truncate"`, `"unlink"`, `"dd"`, `"tar"`).
2. Mirror entry added to `PACK_ENTRIES` in `src/packs/mod.rs` (same string set; the duplicate-source-of-truth is intentional for fast-path quick-reject).
3. Two destructive patterns per command class:
   - `<cmd>-<form>-root-home` (Critical) — sensitive path target.
   - `<cmd>-<form>-general` (High) — anywhere else.
4. Whole-command-anchored safe pattern(s) for benign use under temp dirs (where applicable). NOT segment-aware — see find-delete rationale (segment-aware safes shadow destructive across segments and re-open the bypass class).
5. Suggestion entries in `src/suggestions.rs` registered against both rule IDs.
6. Path-prefix normalization: add the binary to `PATH_NORMALIZER`'s capture group (`(rm|git|find|<new>)`) in `src/normalize.rs` so `/usr/bin/<cmd>` is normalized.

## Cross-cutting concerns

- All children must NOT regress the find-delete patterns or each other's. Run the full lib test suite + integration suite before each merge.
- Each child should add its scenarios to `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh` (new file shared across children) so end-to-end coverage stays consolidated.
- Update goldens after each child if `keywords_checked` arrays are touched.

## Promotion of system.disk to defaults

A separate sub-bead (linked here, not nested) covers promoting `system.disk` to the default-enabled pack set. mkfs / dd to /dev/* / fdisk should not require opt-in for a safety tool.","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Every child bead is closed
- [ ] No regression: full `cargo test` passes (currently 2667 lib + integration tests)
- [ ] No regression: empirical bypass matrix in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh` exits 0
- [ ] Documented in CHANGELOG.md and README.md
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER unit tests cover every newly-added binary","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-04-28T21:06:47.633243718Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T22:00:13.048144104Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

1. Pack `keywords` array updated to include the new binary name (e.g. `"shred"`, `"truncate"`, `"unlink"`, `"dd"`, `"tar"`).
2. Mirror entry added to `PACK_ENTRIES` in `src/packs/mod.rs` (same string set; the duplicate-source-of-truth is intentional for fast-path quick-reject).
3. Two destructive patterns per command class:
   - `<cmd>-<form>-root-home` (Critical) — sensitive path target.
   - `<cmd>-<form>-general` (High) — anywhere else.

A separate sub-bead (linked here, not nested) covers promoting `system.disk` to the default-enabled pack set. mkfs / dd to /dev/* / fdisk should not require opt-in for a safety tool.","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Every child bead is closed

Bypass-family closure landed across ~13 commits over the past day. Final lib test count: 2718. Final e2e harness assertion count: 473 (find=31, unlink=35, truncate=33, shred=33, tar=55, dd=67, mv=60, redirect=68, system_disk_default=29 + harness setup). Two follow-ups spawned: 33xf (cross-segment data-flow analyzer for cp/ln/rsync chains) and 8kh4 (mkswap coverage gap in system.disk). The recursive-force-delete bypass surface is now closed for the canonical sibling commands.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.1","title":"Block truncate -s 0|--size=0|-s -N as recursive-force-delete equivalent","description":"## Description

`truncate -s 0 <file>` zeros a file in place — semantically equivalent to deleting its content. With a sensitive target like `/etc/passwd` or `/etc/shadow`, this is a denial-of-service / data-destruction primitive that bypasses every existing dcg pack. Variants:

- `truncate -s 0 <file>` — zero out
- `truncate --size=0 <file>` — long form
- `truncate -s -<N> <file>` — shrink by N bytes (data loss)
- `truncate -s <N> <file>` where N less than current size — also data loss but harder to detect statically

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"truncate"` (and `"/truncate"`) added to both `core::filesystem::create_pack` keywords AND `PACK_ENTRIES` in `src/packs/mod.rs`.
- Path normalizer: add `truncate` to `(rm|git|find|truncate)` capture in `src/normalize.rs::PATH_NORMALIZER` and `QUOTED_PATH_NORMALIZER`.
- Destructive `truncate-zero-root-home` (Critical): regex matches `\btruncate\b` followed by `-s 0` (or `--size=0`) and a sensitive path token from the standard set (`/`, `/etc`, `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/root`, `/boot`, `/lib`, `/lib64`, `/var`, `/home`, `/sys`, `/proc`, `/dev`, `/opt`, `~`, `$HOME`).
- Destructive `truncate-zero-general` (High): `truncate -s 0 <any-path>` not under a temp dir.
- Destructive `truncate-shrink-root-home` (Critical): `truncate -s -<N>` on sensitive path (negative size = shrink).
- Safe `truncate-tmp` (whole-command anchored): `^truncate\s+(?:-s\s+\S+|--size=\S+)\s+/tmp/...$` and similar for `/var/tmp`, `$TMPDIR`, `${TMPDIR}`.

Severity model mirrors the find-delete family.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 4 patterns ship in `src/packs/core/filesystem.rs` with comments explaining each.
- [ ] `keywords` updated in BOTH `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES` (the duplicate-source-of-truth gotcha).
- [ ] `PATH_NORMALIZER` includes `truncate`, with a unit test pinning the contract.
- [ ] Suggestion entries registered in `src/suggestions.rs` for each new rule ID; coverage tests pass.
- [ ] No regression: full `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh` extended with `scenario_truncate` covering all forms below.
- [ ] Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.

## Notes

### Test scenarios (must all be in scenario_truncate)

Block (Critical):
- `truncate -s 0 /etc/passwd`
- `truncate --size=0 /etc/shadow`
- `truncate -s -100 /etc/hosts`
- `truncate -s 0 ~/.bashrc`
- `truncate -s 0 $HOME/.ssh/authorized_keys`
- `/usr/bin/truncate -s 0 /etc/passwd`     (path-normalized)
- `sudo truncate -s 0 /etc/passwd`         (wrapper-stripped)
- `echo done; truncate -s 0 /etc/passwd`   (compound)

Block (High):
- `truncate -s 0 ./important.db`
- `truncate -s 0 build/output.bin`
- `truncate -s -100 ./large.log`

Allow:
- `truncate -s 0 /tmp/scratch.bin`
- `truncate -s 1G /tmp/sparse-file.bin`     (growing a sparse file is fine)
- `truncate -s 0 $TMPDIR/cache.bin`
- `truncate -s 100M /var/tmp/test.img`
- `cat /etc/truncate-readme.txt`            (substring trap)
- `man truncate`                             (no -s flag)
- `truncate --help`                          (--help)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- With `DCG_BYPASS=1`, `truncate -s 0 /etc/passwd` MUST still block. (defense in depth — confirms the bypass var doesn't disable Critical packs)

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness contract and logging requirements — `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.)

### Out of scope

- `truncate -s +N file` (growing a file) — not destructive.
- `dd of=<file>` — covered by sibling bead.
- `>` redirect truncation — covered by sibling bead.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:01:22.249353876Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T23:24:29.777310580Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T23:24:29.776887768Z","close_reason":"Implemented truncate-zero-root-home + truncate-zero-general for -s 0/--size=0/-s -N forms. 6 safe patterns (temp dirs, --help/--version, +N grow). Added truncate to PACK_ENTRIES + PATH_NORMALIZER. Wired suggestions. 7 unit tests + 5 e2e scenarios. 148/148 e2e pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:01:22.249353876Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.2","title":"Block shred -u/--remove as recursive-force-delete equivalent","description":"## Description

`shred -u <file>` (and especially `shred -fzu <file>`) overwrites then unlinks a file. On a sensitive target this is irrecoverable destruction equivalent to recursive force-delete. Today dcg has no shred coverage.

Variants:
- `shred -u <file>`            — overwrite + unlink
- `shred -fzu <file>`          — force + zero-pass + unlink (most aggressive)
- `shred --remove <file>`      — long form for -u
- `shred -n <N> -u <file>`     — N overwrite passes

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"shred"` and `"/shred"` in `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- PATH_NORMALIZER: add `shred` to capture group in BOTH normalizers.
- Destructive `shred-unlink-root-home` (Critical): regex `\bshred\b` + a flag word containing `u` (e.g. `-u`, `-fu`, `-fzu`, `--remove`) + sensitive path.
- Destructive `shred-unlink-general` (High): same but for any path outside temp dirs.
- Safe `shred-tmp` (whole-command anchored): `^shred\s+...\s+/tmp/...$` and `/var/tmp`, `$TMPDIR`, `${TMPDIR}` variants.

Note: `shred <file>` WITHOUT `-u` only overwrites — the file still exists (zeroed). That is a destructive write but not a delete. We handle it under `shred-overwrite-*` rules at the same severity tiers (Critical for sensitive, High for general). When `-u`/`--remove` is added, the file is also unlinked — same severity, different rule name for explainability.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 4 destructive patterns + 4 safe patterns in `src/packs/core/filesystem.rs`.
- [ ] Keyword in `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated and unit-tested.
- [ ] Suggestion entries registered in `src/suggestions.rs`.
- [ ] No regression: `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] `scenario_shred` added to `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `shred -u /etc/passwd`
- `shred -fzu /etc/shadow`
- `shred --remove /etc/hosts`
- `shred -n 3 -u /etc/passwd`
- `/usr/bin/shred -fzu /etc/passwd`
- `sudo shred -u /etc/passwd`
- `echo done && shred -u /etc/passwd`

Block (High):
- `shred -u ./secrets.txt`
- `shred -fzu ~/Documents/private.bin`         (~ is technically sensitive — should this be Critical? Decide and document.)
- `shred -u build/output.bin`
- `shred secrets.txt`                           (overwrite-only, no -u → block as overwrite-general)

Allow:
- `shred -u /tmp/scratch.bin`
- `shred -fzu $TMPDIR/cache.bin`
- `shred --help`
- `man shred`
- `cat /etc/shred-readme.txt`                   (substring trap)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 shred -u /etc/passwd` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness contract.)

### Decision needed (block in design phase)

- `shred -u ~/...` — is `~` Critical-tier (parity with rm-rf-root-home)? Recommendation: YES. Document.
- `shred -u <relative-path-on-the-filesystem>` — only High because relative paths often refer to scratch.

### Out of scope

- `wipefs <device>` — that's system.disk territory.","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.

Wired suggestions. 6 unit tests + 5 e2e scenarios. 196/196 e2e pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:02:07.236372359Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.3","title":"Block unlink as recursive-force-delete equivalent for single files","description":"## Description

`unlink <file>` is the raw POSIX unlink(2) — equivalent to `rm <file>` (no `-r`, single file). Today dcg has no `unlink` coverage. Combined with a sensitive target like `/etc/passwd`, this is a one-shot data destruction primitive.

Variants:
- `unlink <file>`           — POSIX unlink builtin / GNU coreutils binary
- `python3 -c 'os.unlink("...")'` — already covered by heredoc.python rules

## Design

Add to `core.filesystem`:

- Keyword: `"unlink"` and `"/unlink"` in `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- PATH_NORMALIZER: add `unlink` to capture group in BOTH normalizers.
- Destructive `unlink-root-home` (Critical): regex `\bunlink\s+` + sensitive path.
- Destructive `unlink-general` (High): `unlink <any-path>` not under temp.
- Safe `unlink-tmp` (whole-command anchored): `^unlink\s+/tmp/...$` and the standard temp variants.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 2 destructive + 4 safe patterns in `src/packs/core/filesystem.rs`.
- [ ] Keyword in BOTH `create_pack` AND `PACK_ENTRIES`.
- [ ] PATH_NORMALIZER updated + unit-tested.
- [ ] Suggestion entries.
- [ ] No regression: `cargo test --lib` passes.
- [ ] `scenario_unlink` in `scripts/e2e_destructive_equivalents.sh`.

## Notes

### Test scenarios

Block (Critical):
- `unlink /etc/passwd`
- `unlink /etc/shadow`
- `unlink ~/.bashrc`
- `unlink $HOME/.ssh/id_ed25519`
- `/usr/bin/unlink /etc/passwd`
- `sudo unlink /etc/passwd`
- `echo done; unlink /etc/passwd`

Block (High):
- `unlink ./important.db`
- `unlink build/critical.bin`

Allow:
- `unlink /tmp/scratch.bin`
- `unlink $TMPDIR/cache.bin`
- `unlink --help`
- `cat /etc/unlink-readme.md`           (substring trap)
- `find / -newer foo -unlink-this-fake-flag`  (substring trap)

DCG_BYPASS guard:
- `DCG_BYPASS=1 unlink /etc/passwd` MUST still block.

(See parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi for the shared e2e harness.)

### Out of scope

- `unlink(2)` syscalls inside compiled binaries — out of dcg scope (we block at exec, not at syscall).","acceptance_criteria":"See the design notes for full acceptance criteria. Summary checklist (must all be ticked before close):

- Implementation matches design notes.
- All new unit tests pass (`cargo test --lib`).
- All new integration tests pass (`cargo test --tests`).
- E2E script executes cleanly with structured logging (per parent epic git_safety_guard-nqhi shared contract).
- No regression in existing test suite.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.
- `cargo fmt --check` clean.
- CHANGELOG.md updated.
- Goldens regenerated if `keywords_checked` arrays drift.
- Code reviewed by at least one other agent or human.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:02:29.508812052Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-28T23:15:29.220354841Z","closed_at":"2026-04-28T23:15:29.219982002Z","close_reason":"Implemented unlink-root-home + unlink-general patterns with 4 temp safe whitelists. Wired suggestions. 8 unit tests + 5 e2e scenarios all green. 94/94 e2e pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi.3","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-nqhi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-28T22:02:29.508812052Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
